channel.title: NYT > Business
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Confusion and Fright at the White House Correspondents’ Dinner After Shots Were Fired
item.description: A sense of danger spread like a wave among high-profile politicians and journalists as an emergency unfolded at the White House Correspondents’ Association dinner.
item.dc:creator: Michael M. Grynbaum
item.media:credit: Salwan Georges for The New York Times
item.media:description: Secret Service members responding after the sound of gunfire at the Washington Hilton on Saturday.

item.title: ‘Michael’ is Expected to Collect $200 Million in First Weekend, Shaking Off Bad Reviews
item.description: Lionsgate estimated on Saturday that the Michael Jackson biopic would collect more than $200 million over its first few days in theaters.
item.dc:creator: Brooks Barnes
item.media:credit: Lionsgate
item.media:description: Weekend ticket sales in North America for “Michael” could reach $95 million. Box office analysts had expected about $70 million.

item.title: A Year Later, Trump’s ‘Most Exclusive’ Memecoin Event Is a Lot Less Exclusive
item.description: Even a Times reporter qualified for the event, which caused outrage last year for providing access to President Trump in exchange for investment in one of his family’s crypto ventures.
item.dc:creator: David Yaffe-Bellany
item.media:credit: Tim Enthoven

item.title: In Wine Country, Sales Are Down and Fraud Is Rampant
item.description: The industry’s murky supply chain has long attracted scammers and con artists. In the words of one expert, “Wine and fraud go hand in hand.”
item.dc:creator: Ian Frisch
item.media:credit: Joseph Gough

item.title: Thousands of EV Car Leases Are Ending Soon, Giving Buyers a More Affordable Option
item.description: The leases on hundreds of thousands of battery-powered cars and trucks will end in the next three years, and many will end up on used-car lots.
item.dc:creator: Neal E. Boudette
item.media:credit: Justin Sullivan/Getty Images
item.media:description: Analysts say lower prices of used electric cars and the surging cost of gasoline will encourage more people to consider models that they may have found too expensive earlier.

item.title: If On Shoes Are for Everyone, Are They Still Ons?
item.description: The company finds itself at a critical juncture that others have botched: How does it stay a brand for serious athletes while keeping up its breakneck growth?
item.dc:creator: Jordyn Holman
item.media:credit: Clara Watt for The New York Times
item.media:description: On’s LIghtSpray Cloudboom Strike running shoe — on display, museum-style, at the company’s Zurich headquarters — was made with a technology that mists the upper part of the shoe onto the sole.

item.title: SiriusXM Said to Be in Early Talks to Acquire iHeartMedia
item.description: A deal, which was described by two people familiar with the discussions, would unite two major providers of traditional and satellite radio.
item.dc:creator: Lauren Hirsch and Benjamin Mullin
item.media:credit: Amy Lombard for The New York Times
item.media:description: The hosts of “SmartLess,” from left, Jason Bateman, Will Arnett and Sean Hayes, on a sofa after a taping in 2024. SiriusXM’s chief executive, Jennifer C. Witz, and chief content officer, Scott Greenstein, stood behind them.

item.title: 31 Sloths Acquired by an Orlando Animal Attraction Died, Officials Say
item.description: Many of the deaths were attributed to a lack of heat at an unauthorized warehouse that Sloth World was using, officials said.
item.dc:creator: Neil Vigdor
item.media:credit: Fox 35 Orlando
item.media:description: The sloths were going to be the latest attraction at Sloth World in Orlando, Fla.

item.title: The ‘Lasting Damage’ of Pirro’s Investigation of the Federal Reserve and Powell
item.description: The Trump administration’s attacks on the Federal Reserve have rattled confidence in the central bank’s ability to operate independently before a leadership transition.
item.dc:creator: Colby Smith and Tony Romm
item.media:credit: Haiyun Jiang/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump visited the Federal Reserve’s Washington headquarters in July during renovations.

item.title: Over 200,000 Heated Socks Sold at Costco Recalled After Reports of Burns
item.description: The socks, from the brand 32 Degrees, were linked to reports of first- and second-degree burns, according to U.S. product safety regulators.
item.dc:creator: Vjosa Isai
item.media:credit: Graham Dickie/The New York Times
item.media:description: The recalled socks were sold exclusively at Costco, where customers can return them and seek refunds, the company said in its recall notice.

item.title: Inside Trump and David Ellison’s Private Party in Washington
item.description: The gathering included executives and leading journalists from CBS News, which Paramount owns, and the head of the Justice Department, which is reviewing the acquisition.
item.dc:creator: Michael M. Grynbaum
item.media:credit: Jeenah Moon/Reuters

item.title: Judge Imposes Sanctions on Plaintiff and Her Lawyer in Suit Against Billionaire Leon Black
item.description: A federal judge said the lawyer representing an unnamed plaintiff who accused the billionaire investor of abusing her had “lied repeatedly to the court.”
item.dc:creator: Matthew Goldstein
item.media:credit: Lucy Nicholson/Reuters
item.media:description: In a lawsuit, a woman said Leon Black sexually abused her when she was a teenager. He has denied all accusations.

item.title: How Jet Fuel Shortages Could Affect Summer Travel to Europe and Beyond
item.description: Facing sky-high fuel costs linked to the war in Iran, airlines are cutting routes and raising prices. European vacations are looking a lot less affordable.
item.dc:creator: Christine Chung
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Kirill Kudryavtsev/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: The German airline Lufthansa cut nearly 20,000 flights over the next six months to conserve jet fuel, which could begin to run low in Europe as soon as next month.

item.title: Trump Says He Dislikes Prediction Markets. His Family Invests in Them.
item.description: The White House has warned staff not to wager on government decisions, but his family’s involvement with these firms undermines the president’s message.
item.dc:creator: Ben Protess
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump’s eldest son, Donald Trump Jr., has ties to two of the industry’s top prediction market firms, including Polymarket, a platform that prosecutors say a U.S. soldier used to place bets using classified information.

item.title: What Are Prediction Markets, and Why Are They Causing Controversy?
item.description: The indictment of a soldier who bet on the U.S. operation to capture President Nicolás Maduro of Venezuela put renewed focus on a new way to gamble, and a new way to cheat.
item.dc:creator: Victor Mather
item.media:credit: Jenny Kane/Associated Press
item.media:description: Prediction markets like Kalshi and Polymarket are a growing phenomenon.

item.title: $450 Million Worth of Newhouse Trophies Come to Christie’s
item.description: A special May evening sale will feature 16 artworks from the museum-quality collection of the Condé Nast chief S.I. Newhouse Jr.
item.dc:creator: Robin Pogrebin
item.media:credit: Succession Brancusi/All rights reserved (Adagp); Photo by George Etheredge for The New York Times
item.media:description: A Brancusi head in the collection of S.I. Newhouse Jr., expected to sell for $100 million, is decorated with gold leaf.

item.title: As Trump Officials Pushed Health Savings Accounts, RFK Jr. Aide Ran Wellness Company Poised to Benefit
item.description: Calley Means remained president of a company that relied on health savings accounts last year as the Trump administration developed policies to expand them.
item.dc:creator: Christina Jewett and Benjamin Mueller
item.media:credit: Al Drago for The New York Times
item.media:description: Last year, Calley Means was a special government employee, a class of workers that can retain their business interests while working in government.

item.title: It Was Just a Podcast. Now, It’s Kelce Land.
item.description: When Amazon gutted its podcast company, it built a new department that made creators kings, starting with the football stars Jason and Travis Kelce.
item.dc:creator: Jessica Testa
item.media:credit: Balazs Gardi for The New York Times

item.title: Polymarket Bets on Paris Temperature Prompt Investigation After Unusual Spikes
item.description: The authorities said a Paris airport weather sensor may have been tampered with as large wagers were placed on the betting site.
item.dc:creator: Jonathan Wolfe and Ana Castelain
item.media:credit: Francois Mori/Associated Press
item.media:description: Paris Charles de Gaulle Airport, the main airport in Paris.

item.title: The Peril of Piloting Ships Through the Strait of Hormuz
item.description: Controllers for shipping companies face a daunting decision as they try to free vessels trapped in the Persian Gulf: “Basically you’re sending someone unarmed into war.”
item.dc:creator: Jenny Gross
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Maximilian Mann for The New York Times
item.media:description: Silke Lehmköster oversees 310 vessels as the fleet managing director at Hapag-Lloyd, a shipping giant in Hamburg, Germany.

item.title: Justice Department Drops Criminal Investigation of Fed Chair Jerome Powell
item.description: The decision to end the inquiry into Jerome H. Powell’s handling of the Federal Reserve’s renovation could allow Kevin M. Warsh, the president’s pick for Fed chair, to be confirmed.
item.dc:creator: Colby Smith, Tony Romm and Alan Feuer
item.media:credit: Caroline Gutman for The New York Times
item.media:description: Jerome H. Powell, the Federal Reserve chair.

item.title: How Kevin Warsh Could Shrink the Fed’s Footprint in Financial Markets
item.description: President Trump’s nominee to become the next chair of the Federal Reserve wants to overhaul the central bank, including its more than $6 trillion balance sheet.
item.dc:creator: Colby Smith
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times
item.media:description: “It took 18 years to create this balance sheet problem, and we won’t be able to fix it in 18 minutes,” Kevin M. Warsh, the Federal Reserve chair nominee, told lawmakers on Tuesday.

item.title: Why Everyone, Including Trump, Is Talking About Airline Mergers
item.description: A few carriers are making a lot of money while many are struggling. President Trump and industry executives seem to think mergers may be the answer.
item.dc:creator: Niraj Chokshi
item.media:credit: Dakota Santiago for The New York Times
item.media:description: After years of mounting costs, thin profits and a greater emphasis on selling premium seats, the airline industry is buzzing about possible mergers, partnerships and a government bailout.

item.title: Ignoring the War Has Been Working for Long-Term Investors
item.description: The U.S. stock market’s quick rebound this month may be fragile, but riding out the market’s disconcerting waves has been a sound strategy.
item.dc:creator: Jeff Sommer
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi for The New York Times
item.media:description: Tehran in the early days of the war. For investors who have held broad index funds that mirror the market, it’s been a decent year so far.

item.title: A.I. Is Forcing More Belt-Tightening at Big Tech
item.description: The growing expense of keeping up in the artificial intelligence race is driving technology companies, young and old, to cut costs.
item.dc:creator: Andrew Ross Sorkin, Bernhard Warner, Sarah Kessler, Michael J. de la Merced, Niko Gallogly, Brian O’Keefe and Ian Mount
item.media:credit: Mike Kai Chen for The New York Times
item.media:description: Tech companies like OpenAI, and its C.E.O., Sam Altman, are under increasing pressure to rethink costs.

item.title: A.I. Start-Ups From Canada and Germany Merge to Take On Silicon Valley
item.description: Cohere is acquiring Aleph Alpha in a deal aimed at customers uneasy about the dominance of American companies in artificial intelligence.
item.dc:creator: Adam Satariano
item.media:credit: Brendan Ko for The New York Times
item.media:description: Nick Frosst, left, and Ivan Zhang, co-founders of Cohere, in 2022. The start-up was seen as one of the few companies to challenge its Silicon Valley rivals.

item.title: Tariffs Raised Consumers’ Prices, but the Refunds Go Only to Businesses
item.description: Many families felt the sting of the president’s now-illegal tariffs, but companies have said little about whether they will share the $166 billion coming back to them.
item.dc:creator: Tony Romm
item.media:credit: Mark Abramson for The New York Times
item.media:description: The biggest beneficiaries of tariff refunds may include retail giants like Costco, even if companies passed the costs onto their customers.

item.title: Who’s Getting a Tariff Refund?
item.description: Following a Supreme Court ruling that struck down several Trump administration tariffs, importers have begun applying for their share of $166 billion in refunds. As our economic policy reporter Tony Romm explains, consumers are unlikely to see much of that money returned to their own pockets.
item.dc:creator: Tony Romm, Nour Idriss, Stephanie Swart, Whitney Shefte and Paul Abowd

item.title: Sam Altman’s Next High-Wire Act: Getting OpenAI to Make More Money
item.description: Mr. Altman, who has faced criticism over OpenAI’s direction, has culled company projects and is trying to be more disciplined with strategy.
item.dc:creator: Mike Isaac
item.media:credit: Photo Illustration by Javier Palma; Source Photographs by Rod Lamkey Jr. for The New York Times; Karsten Moran for The New York Times; Getty

item.title: Where to Enjoy an Airport Shower After a Long Flight
item.description: Affordable arrivals lounges offer amenities like nap pods and showers where weary passengers can freshen up. You may never start a vacation the same way again.
item.dc:creator: Julie Weed
item.media:credit: Liam Cobb

item.title: How Elon Musk Used SpaceX to Benefit Himself and His Businesses
item.description: The rocket maker has been a useful financial tool for Mr. Musk, providing the billionaire with loans and aiding his struggling companies, a Times examination found.
item.dc:creator: Susanne Craig and Kirsten Grind
item.media:credit: David Plunkert

item.title: Who’s Who in ‘Michael’: A Guide to the Characters
item.description: A new Michael Jackson biopic, with the star’s nephew Jaafar Jackson in the title role, is packed with famous musical figures. These are some of them.
item.dc:creator: Sarah Bahr

item.title: DeepSeek’s Sequel Set to Extend China’s Reach in Open-Source A.I.
item.description: Chinese companies have embraced making their most advanced artificial intelligence models available to all.
item.dc:creator: Meaghan Tobin and Cade Metz
item.media:credit: Kelsey McClellan for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Chinese start-up DeepSeek shook the industry in January 2025 with its claim that it had created an advanced A.I. system for a fraction of what its American rivals spent.

item.title: War and Sanctions Accelerate China’s Currency Push
item.description: China’s bid to build a renminbi-based financial system beyond the U.S. dollar’s reach is gaining traction as a way to sidestep sanctions.
item.dc:creator: Aaron Krolik
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Lincoln Agnew

item.title: Pentagon Fires Stars and Stripes Newspaper’s Ombudsman
item.description: The newspaper’s ombudsman, Jacqueline Smith, said she had been given no reason for her dismissal.
item.dc:creator: Erik Wemple
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Valerie Plesch for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Pentagon has been taking steps to increase its editorial control of Stars and Stripes, which has covered the military continuously since World War II.

item.title: Soldier Used Classified Information to Bet on Maduro’s Ouster, U.S. Says
item.description: Federal prosecutors say that Sgt. Gannon Ken Van Dyke, who was involved in the operation to oust Nicolás Maduro from power in Venezuela, used the information to place bets on a prediction market.
item.dc:creator: Benjamin Weiser and Jonah E. Bromwich
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Vincent Alban/The New York Times
item.media:description: Nicolás Maduro, former President of Venezuela, being escorted off a helicopter to be taken to the federal courthouse in Manhattan in January.

item.title: Nike to Cut 1,400 Jobs as Part of Its Turnaround Plan
item.description: Most of the layoffs will affect tech workers as the athletic giant tries to reverse a yearslong sales slump. It’s the second round of cuts this year.
item.dc:creator: Kim Bhasin
item.media:credit: Gabby Jones for The New York Times
item.media:description: Nike said on Thursday that layoffs would affect workers across North America, Europe and Asia.

item.title: Microsoft Targets About 7% of Its U.S. Workers With Buyout Offer
item.description: The tech giant is offering long-serving employees early retirements as it continues to invest aggressively in artificial intelligence.
item.dc:creator: Karen Weise
item.media:credit: Grant Hindsley for The New York Times
item.media:description: Microsoft and other big tech companies are on a spending spree to build the infrastructure for artificial intelligence.

item.title: Two Litigation Partners Depart Paul Weiss
item.description: Kannon Shanmugam and Masha Hansford are the latest in a string of litigators who have left the influential New York law firm.
item.dc:creator: Jessica Silver-Greenberg and Michael S. Schmidt
item.media:credit: Andrew Kelly/Reuters
item.media:description: The Paul Weiss partners Kannon Shanmugam and Masha Hansford are departing to start a practice focusing on Supreme Court and other appellate litigation at the rival firm Davis Polk.

item.title: New Gene Therapy Enables Children With a Rare Form of Deafness to Hear
item.description: The treatment, the first of its kind, was approved by the Food and Drug Administration on Thursday. “Our baby was born deaf, and now he can hear,” said one parent.
item.dc:creator: Gina Kolata
item.media:credit: Christopher Capozziello for The New York Times
item.media:description: Miles was born with otoferlin deafness, a rare form of deafness caused by a mutation in a single gene. But with a new gene therapy, he can hear.

item.title: Congress May Finally Recognize That Mobile Homes Aren’t Really That Mobile
item.description: An obscure federal rule requires manufactured homes to be built on a chassis, making them more costly. A bill in Congress would remove the mandate, enacted five decades ago.
item.dc:creator: Matthew Goldstein
item.media:credit: Carlos Bernate for The New York Times
item.media:description: Manufactured homes being installed in Petersburg, Va. A 1970s law requires all manufactured homes to have a steel chassis, a rule from the days when these structures were called mobile homes.

item.title: How We Cover the White House Correspondents’ Dinner
item.description: The annual black-tie event attracts Hollywood stars, reporters and politicians for an irreverent night celebrating press freedom.
item.dc:creator: Sarah Bahr
item.media:credit: Haiyun Jiang for The New York Times
item.media:description: Politicians, Hollywood stars and journalists representing more than 100 news organizations attend the White House Correspondents’ Association dinner in Washington. Above, the scene in 2024.

item.title: Warner Bros. Discovery Shareholders Approve Paramount Deal
item.description: The vote brings the tech scion David Ellison one step closer to uniting some of the world’s biggest news and entertainment properties.
item.dc:creator: Benjamin Mullin
item.media:credit: Mark Abramson for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Warner Bros. Studio in Burbank, Calif. The vote on Thursday vote does not end the wrangling over the deal Paramount’s deal for Warner Bros.

item.title: Investors Fret Over Tesla’s $25 Billion A.I. Bet
item.description: Elon Musk’s electric vehicle company said that it needs to spend big on artificial intelligence and robots. Wall Street is worried about the bill.
item.dc:creator: Andrew Ross Sorkin, Bernhard Warner, Sarah Kessler, Michael J. de la Merced, Niko Gallogly, Brian O’Keefe, Michelle Leder and Lauren Hirsch
item.media:credit: Go Nakamura/Reuters
item.media:description: Tesla’s giant bet on artificial intelligence, humanoid robots and more isn’t going over well with investors this morning.

item.title: Why Diesel Has Become a Much Bigger Economic Problem Than Gasoline
item.description: The war in Iran has disrupted supplies of diesel, used to power trucks and heavy equipment, much more than gasoline, which is primarily used in passenger cars.
item.dc:creator: Emmett Lindner
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
Far East, South and Southeast Asia and Pacific Areas
item.media:credit: Adam Amengual for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Port of Los Angeles. Large semi trucks and some ships run on diesel fuel.

item.title: As U.S. Brands Stumble, China Wins Over Young Indonesians
item.description: A new generation of Indonesian consumers view Chinese brands as high-tech and of good quality, a departure from the days when “made in China” was associated with cheap goods.
item.dc:creator: Alexandra Stevenson and Hasya Nindita
item.media:credit: Ulet Ifansasti for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Glodok market in the Chinatown area of Jakarta, Indonesia’s capital, decked out for Lunar New Year in February.

item.title: Controversies and Production Issues Around ‘Michael,’ the Jackson Biopic, Explained
item.description: Reshoots, reluctant studios and family holdouts: the production faced many issues. But now the box office is expected to be huge.
item.dc:creator: Brooks Barnes
item.media:credit: Glen Wilson/Lionsgate, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Jaafar Jackson as Michael Jackson, his uncle, in the new biopic “Michael.”

item.title: Some Labubu Dolls Contain Cotton Banned by Forced Labor Law, Testing Shows
item.description: A test verified by The New York Times found that clothes for some of the viral dolls contained cotton from the Xinjiang region of China, which is banned in the United States.
item.dc:creator: Ana Swanson, Sapna Maheshwari and Meaghan Tobin
item.category: Human Rights and Human Rights Violations
item.media:credit: Jared Soares for The New York Times
item.media:description: For businesses like Pop Mart, selling products that contain cotton banned in the United States could have severe consequences.

item.title: Oil Prices Rise and Stocks Slide Amid Uncertainty Over Next Steps in War
item.description: Investors wrestled with uncertainty about the cease-fire between the United States and Iran.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Justin Tallis/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
